games.explanation: Bradish's surface numbers look terrifying until you notice he's been a different pitcher against lefties, and Tampa happens to roll out a lineup built to exploit exactly that split. Aranda and Diaz from the left side are the kind of patient, hard-contact bats that turn his 12% walk rate to lefties into actual damage, and Caminero is there to punish anything that drifts middle. Jax has been the steadier arm under the hood despite the noisier ERA, and the Elo gap here is enormous for a game priced this close to a coin flip. At -124 you're getting the clearly better team against a small-sample starter the matchup is built to neutralize.

Chase Burns has a strikeout pitch and a real problem, which is that left-handed bats have been teeing off on him every time he takes the mound. Philadelphia happens to run out the worst possible lineup for that flaw, with Schwarber and Harper headlining a group that's slugged a .383 wOBA against him from the left side. Luzardo is the steadier arm in this matchup by a comfortable margin, and a hot Cincinnati night with the wind blowing out cuts in Schwarber's favor far more than it does anyone in red. -142 is a fair toll for the better team, the better starter, and the platoon mismatch all stacked on the same ticket.

Nolan McLean's overall line looks like an ace until you split it by handedness, and the Nats happen to roll out a lineup built to attack the side where he actually bleeds. Against righties he's been a different pitcher, surrendering barrels at a 12.5% clip while his command tightens into the strike zone, which is precisely the diet Wood, García and Abrams have been feasting on all year. The sim has this as a one-run game, and one-run games at plus money are how you get paid for being right about a coin flip. Washington isn't the better team, but +130 isn't asking them to be.

Mitch Keller has spent the season looking like a perfectly average mid-rotation arm against righties and a batting practice machine against lefties, and the Cardinals happen to be built to exploit exactly that. Burleson from the left side and a lineup that can stack platoon advantages turns Keller into the kind of starter who gives up a crooked number in the third and never quite recovers. The board is treating this like a coin flip because Liberatore is unspectacular, but a 7-point edge on a home favorite getting plus-ish juice is the spot you take every time. At -108, you're paying flat money for the better team in the better matchup.

Reid Detmers is the lefty the market keeps treating like he's solved right-handed hitters, and the Athletics roll out a lineup full of righties who don't strike out and do damage against southpaws. Langeliers and Rooker both jump a tier against lefties, and Detmers has quietly been getting barreled up by righty bats at an 11 percent clip despite the shiny strikeout number. Lopez isn't a star, but he's a tougher matchup for this Angels lineup than Detmers is for that A's order, and the wind blowing in at 12 mph trims the Trout-and-Neto homer equity the Halos are leaning on. Getting plus money on the better team in a game the board has flipped is the entire pitch.

Emmet Sheehan misses bats and posts a shiny ERA, but he hands out free passes to lefties at a rate that turns short outings into bullpen games, and the Padres happen to roll out the kind of patient right-handed bats that don't bail him out either. Tatis and Machado have been punishing right-handers all year, with Tatis sitting on a .375 xwOBA against the hand, and Sheehan's barrel rate to lefties is the soft underbelly Gavin Sheets is built to attack. Canning isn't a world-beater, but the Elo gap tilts San Diego's way and the projected score is a one-run fight, not the mismatch the board is pricing. Getting +135 on the better team in a coin-flip game is the entire argument.

Bryce Miller is the kind of starter who gets propped up by his uniform, because nobody actually checks whether the contact against him has been loud. It has been, with a hard-hit rate near 48% that suggests the White Sox lineup, light as it looks on paper, will find barrels tonight. Montgomery in particular is the exact left-handed profile that punishes this arm, and T-Mobile with the roof shut still plays as a pitcher's park that keeps the Mariners offense honest. The board is pricing this like Seattle is the clearly better team, and they aren't. +128 on a coin flip is the entire argument.
